effect: Increased bleeding risk
clinical_consequence: Concurrent use significantly increases risk of major and fatal bleeding including GI hemorrhage and intracranial bleeding. Both inhibit platelet function; warfarin prolongs PT/INR.
timing_notes: If combination unavoidable, use lowest effective aspirin dose with very close INR monitoring

effect: Significantly reduced warfarin plasma levels → loss of anticoagulation → thrombotic events
clinical_consequence: St. John's Wort is a potent CYP2C9 and P-gp inducer. Reduces warfarin AUC by 25-40%. Can precipitate stroke or DVT in patients who rely on therapeutic anticoagulation.
timing_notes: Avoid all products containing Hypericum perforatum

effect: Additive anticoagulant and antiplatelet effects → increased bleeding risk
clinical_consequence: Omega-3 fatty acids inhibit platelet aggregation and may potentiate warfarin. Doses >3g/day show clinically significant INR elevation in some patients.
timing_notes: Monitor INR more frequently when initiating or stopping fish oil

effect: Modest increase in warfarin levels via CYP1A2 inhibition
clinical_consequence: Grapefruit primarily inhibits CYP3A4; warfarin (S-enantiomer) is mainly CYP2C9. Effect on warfarin is generally minor but consistent INR monitoring is advised.
timing_notes: Avoid large quantities of grapefruit juice

effect: Dramatically increased simvastatin plasma levels → rhabdomyolysis, myopathy
clinical_consequence: Grapefruit inhibits intestinal CYP3A4, the primary enzyme metabolizing simvastatin. Even 200-250mL daily can increase simvastatin AUC by >3-fold. Large amounts (>1L/day) increase AUC 7-16x, causing severe myopathy and rhabdomyolysis.
timing_notes: Avoid grapefruit entirely with simvastatin; effect persists >24h after grapefruit consumption

effect: Serotonin syndrome — potentially fatal
clinical_consequence: Combined serotonergic stimulation causes massive serotonin excess: hyperthermia, rigidity, myoclonus, autonomic instability, seizures, death. Onset within hours. MAOI washout: 14 days before SSRI. Fluoxetine requires 5-week washout before MAOI.
timing_notes: MAOI: wait 14 days after stopping before starting SSRI. Fluoxetine: 5-week washout required before starting MAOI

effect: Serotonin syndrome + lowered seizure threshold
clinical_consequence: Tramadol inhibits serotonin reuptake and is a weak MAOI. Combined with SSRIs creates significant serotonin excess. Additionally, CYP2D6 inhibiting SSRIs (fluoxetine, paroxetine) reduce tramadol conversion to active metabolite M1, paradoxically reducing analgesia while increasing parent compound toxicity.
timing_notes: If unavoidable, use lowest effective tramadol dose with close monitoring

effect: Serotonin syndrome
clinical_consequence: St. John's Wort inhibits neuronal serotonin, dopamine, and norepinephrine reuptake. Combined with SSRIs causes additive serotonin excess. Documented cases of serotonin syndrome with agitation, confusion, tremor, diaphoresis.
timing_notes: Avoid concurrent use entirely

effect: Lactic acidosis risk — potentially fatal
clinical_consequence: Alcohol inhibits hepatic gluconeogenesis and lactate clearance. Metformin already predisposes to lactate accumulation. Heavy alcohol use combined with metformin significantly increases lactic acidosis risk, especially in fasting state.
timing_notes: Avoid heavy or binge alcohol use; moderate use with food may be acceptable

effect: Hyperkalemia — cardiac arrhythmias, cardiac arrest
clinical_consequence: ACE inhibitors reduce aldosterone → decreased potassium excretion. Adding exogenous potassium can cause dangerous hyperkalemia (K+ >6.5 mEq/L), causing peaked T-waves, VF, asystole.
timing_notes: Check serum K+ before and regularly during combined therapy

effect: Chelation → dramatically reduced ciprofloxacin absorption (up to 54% reduction)
clinical_consequence: Divalent cations (Ca2+, Mg2+, Al3+, Fe2+) chelate fluoroquinolones in the GI tract, forming insoluble complexes. This can lead to subtherapeutic antibiotic levels and treatment failure, especially for serious infections.
timing_notes: Take ciprofloxacin 2 hours before or 6 hours after calcium-containing products

effect: Theophylline toxicity — nausea, seizures, arrhythmias
clinical_consequence: Ciprofloxacin is a potent CYP1A2 inhibitor. Theophylline is a CYP1A2 substrate with a narrow therapeutic index. Combined use can increase theophylline levels 2-3x, causing tachycardia, seizures, life-threatening arrhythmias.
timing_notes: Reduce theophylline dose by 30-50% when adding ciprofloxacin; monitor serum levels

effect: Major INR elevation → bleeding
clinical_consequence: Amiodarone and its metabolite (desethylamiodarone) are potent CYP2C9 inhibitors. Inhibits warfarin metabolism → INR can double or triple. Effect is delayed (weeks) and persists long after amiodarone discontinuation due to its very long half-life (40-55 days).
timing_notes: Reduce warfarin dose by 30-50% when amiodarone initiated; monitor INR weekly × 4-6 weeks then monthly

effect: Methotrexate toxicity — bone marrow suppression, hepatotoxicity, mucositis
clinical_consequence: NSAIDs reduce renal blood flow and tubular secretion, impairing methotrexate elimination. Even low-dose methotrexate (rheumatoid arthritis) can accumulate to toxic levels. Bone marrow suppression, hepatitis, and renal failure documented.
timing_notes: If unavoidable, hold NSAIDs for 24-48h around high-dose methotrexate infusions

effect: Lithium toxicity — tremor, confusion, seizures, renal failure
clinical_consequence: NSAIDs inhibit prostaglandin-mediated renal blood flow and reduce tubular lithium excretion, increasing lithium levels by 12-66%. Lithium has a narrow therapeutic index (0.6-1.2 mEq/L); toxicity occurs >1.5 mEq/L.
timing_notes: If NSAID unavoidable, check lithium levels within 5-7 days of starting NSAID

effect: Severe myopathy, rhabdomyolysis
clinical_consequence: Clarithromycin is a potent CYP3A4 inhibitor. Simvastatin and atorvastatin are CYP3A4 substrates. Co-administration can increase statin AUC 5-10x, causing severe myopathy and rhabdomyolysis with risk of acute renal failure.
timing_notes: Hold simvastatin/atorvastatin for duration of clarithromycin course. Use pravastatin or rosuvastatin as alternatives.

effect: QT prolongation → Torsades de Pointes → sudden cardiac death
clinical_consequence: Additive hERG potassium channel blockade prolongs cardiac repolarization. Risk of life-threatening ventricular arrhythmia (TdP). FDA black box warning for azithromycin in cardiac patients.
timing_notes: Consider alternative antibiotics (amoxicillin) in patients on QT-prolonging agents

effect: Digoxin toxicity — nausea, bradycardia, heart block, ventricular arrhythmias
clinical_consequence: Amiodarone inhibits P-gp (renal and biliary digoxin excretion) and displaces digoxin from tissue binding. Digoxin levels increase 70-100% within days. Digoxin has a narrow therapeutic index (0.5-2.0 ng/mL).
timing_notes: Reduce digoxin dose by 50% when amiodarone added; check levels in 1 week

effect: Reduced clopidogrel activation → increased cardiovascular events (MI, stroke)
clinical_consequence: Omeprazole is a potent CYP2C19 inhibitor. Clopidogrel requires CYP2C19-mediated bioactivation. Omeprazole reduces active metabolite formation by ~46%, significantly attenuating platelet inhibition. FDA safety communication issued.
timing_notes: Use pantoprazole (weaker CYP2C19 inhibitor) as preferred PPI with clopidogrel

effect: Reduced levothyroxine absorption → hypothyroidism
clinical_consequence: Calcium carbonate chelates levothyroxine in the GI tract, reducing absorption by 20-40%. Can cause clinical hypothyroidism with fatigue, weight gain, depression. Effect is dose-dependent on calcium dose.
timing_notes: Take levothyroxine on empty stomach, at least 4 hours before or after calcium supplements

effect: Reduced levothyroxine absorption → hypothyroidism
clinical_consequence: Ferrous sulfate and other iron salts chelate levothyroxine, reducing oral absorption by up to 34%. TSH elevation documented in case series.
timing_notes: Separate levothyroxine and iron by at least 4 hours

effect: Acute kidney injury + reduced antihypertensive efficacy
clinical_consequence: NSAIDs inhibit prostaglandin-mediated afferent arteriole vasodilation. In patients reliant on these prostaglandins (heart failure, volume depletion, elderly, CKD), co-administration with ACE inhibitors causes 'triple whammy' nephrotoxicity. Also attenuates antihypertensive effect.
timing_notes: Use acetaminophen as analgesic alternative in ACE inhibitor patients

effect: Profound CNS/respiratory depression → respiratory arrest, death
clinical_consequence: Synergistic CNS depression. Both classes enhance GABAergic inhibition (benzodiazepines) and activate mu-opioid receptors. FDA black box warning. Risk of respiratory arrest increases dramatically. 30% of opioid overdose deaths involve benzodiazepines.
timing_notes: If combination medically necessary, use lowest effective doses, titrate slowly, have naloxone available

effect: Hepatotoxicity — acute liver failure
clinical_consequence: Alcohol induces CYP2E1, increasing NAPQI (toxic acetaminophen metabolite) production while depleting hepatic glutathione. Heavy drinkers (>3 drinks/day) are at significantly elevated risk of liver injury even at standard acetaminophen doses. FDA warning label.
timing_notes: Heavy drinkers should avoid acetaminophen. Occasional drinkers: use minimum effective dose

effect: Additive CNS/respiratory depression → sedation, respiratory arrest
clinical_consequence: Ethanol potentiates GABA-A receptor function synergistically with benzodiazepines. Combined with opioids creates triple CNS/respiratory depression risk. Even first-generation antihistamines (diphenhydramine) combined with alcohol cause dangerous sedation.
timing_notes: Avoid alcohol with any CNS depressant class

effect: INR elevation → major bleeding
clinical_consequence: Fluconazole is a potent CYP2C9 inhibitor. S-warfarin (more potent enantiomer) is primarily metabolized by CYP2C9. INR can increase 2-3 fold within 2-3 days. Severe bleeding events documented.
timing_notes: Consider dose reduction of warfarin 25-50% if fluconazole needed; monitor INR every 1-2 days

effect: Tacrolimus toxicity — nephrotoxicity, neurotoxicity
clinical_consequence: Grapefruit inhibits intestinal CYP3A4 and P-gp. Tacrolimus has a narrow therapeutic index (5-20 ng/mL). Even small amounts of grapefruit can increase tacrolimus levels >2x, causing nephrotoxicity and neurotoxicity in transplant patients.
timing_notes: Absolute avoidance of grapefruit, pomelo, and Seville oranges

effect: Contraceptive failure → unintended pregnancy
clinical_consequence: Rifampin is the most potent inducer of CYP3A4 and P-gp. Reduces ethinylestradiol AUC by up to 52% and progestin by 57%. Breakthrough bleeding and contraceptive failure documented. Effect begins within days and persists 4-8 weeks after rifampin discontinuation.
timing_notes: Use non-hormonal contraception during rifampin therapy and for 8 weeks after stopping

effect: Profound, potentially fatal hypotension
clinical_consequence: Both nitrates and PDE5 inhibitors enhance cGMP-mediated vasodilation. Combination causes synergistic vasodilation → severe hypotension → cardiovascular collapse. Absolute contraindication. FDA black box warning.
timing_notes: Sildenafil/vardenafil: wait 24h after nitrate use. Tadalafil: wait 48h

effect: Reduced valproate levels + increased carbamazepine-10,11-epoxide toxicity
clinical_consequence: Carbamazepine is a potent CYP inducer → reduces valproate levels by 40-50%. Valproate inhibits epoxide hydrolase, increasing toxic carbamazepine metabolite. Double interaction requires careful monitoring of both drug levels.
timing_notes: Monitor both drug serum levels; titrate carefully

effect: Serotonin syndrome — hyperthermia, seizures, death
clinical_consequence: DXM weakly inhibits serotonin reuptake. Combined with MAOIs causes severe serotonin syndrome. Numerous fatal cases documented. Risk extends to all MAOI types including selegiline patches at antidepressant doses.
timing_notes: Do not use DXM within 14 days of MAOI

effect: Hypertensive crisis — severe headache, stroke, death
clinical_consequence: MAOIs prevent metabolic inactivation of catecholamines. Pseudoephedrine (indirect sympathomimetic) triggers massive norepinephrine release → hypertensive crisis. BP can exceed 220/140 mmHg within minutes of dose.
timing_notes: Do not use sympathomimetics within 14 days of MAOI

effect: Severe hyperkalemia → cardiac arrest
clinical_consequence: Spironolactone is a potassium-sparing diuretic via aldosterone antagonism. Adding potassium supplementation dramatically increases hyperkalemia risk. K+ >6.5 mEq/L causes peaked T-waves, prolonged PR, wide QRS, ventricular fibrillation.
timing_notes: Discontinue potassium supplements; monitor K+ levels closely

effect: Anticholinergic syndrome — delirium, urinary retention, ileus, hyperthermia
clinical_consequence: Additive anticholinergic effects. Particularly dangerous in elderly. Causes cognitive impairment, dry mouth, urinary retention, constipation, tachycardia, hyperthermia. Anticholinergic burden score elevated → delirium risk.
timing_notes: Use Beers Criteria to minimize anticholinergic burden in elderly patients

effect: Increased risk of tendon rupture — Achilles tendon rupture
clinical_consequence: Both fluoroquinolones and corticosteroids independently increase tendon rupture risk. Combination increases risk 6-fold versus fluoroquinolone alone. Particularly Achilles tendon. Risk increases with age, renal impairment, transplant.
timing_notes: Advise patients to report tendon pain; consider alternative antibiotics if possible

effect: Chelation → reduced fluoroquinolone absorption and efficacy
clinical_consequence: Divalent cations chelate fluoroquinolones in GI tract. Magnesium reduces ciprofloxacin absorption by up to 26%. Can cause antibiotic treatment failure.
timing_notes: Take fluoroquinolone 2 hours before or 6 hours after magnesium

effect: Reduced methotrexate-related toxicity (when used therapeutically)
clinical_consequence: Folate supplementation reduces methotrexate side effects (mucositis, hepatotoxicity, nausea) in rheumatoid arthritis without significantly reducing efficacy. However, high-dose folic acid may theoretically reduce anticancer efficacy of methotrexate.
timing_notes: In RA: take folic acid on non-methotrexate days or >24h after methotrexate. In cancer: avoid without oncologist guidance.

effect: Reduced anticoagulant effect of warfarin
clinical_consequence: Warfarin inhibits vitamin K-dependent clotting factor synthesis (II, VII, IX, X). Increased dietary vitamin K directly antagonizes this effect. Large fluctuations in green vegetable intake (kale, spinach, broccoli) cause unpredictable INR swings and loss of anticoagulant control.
timing_notes: Maintain consistent, not necessarily low, vitamin K intake. Do not dramatically change dietary habits.

effect: Serotonin syndrome risk
clinical_consequence: SSRIs/SNRIs increase synaptic serotonin; triptans are serotonin 5-HT1 receptor agonists. Risk of serotonin syndrome is documented but lower than with MAOI combinations. FDA warning issued in 2006. Monitor for tremor, agitation, diaphoresis.
timing_notes: Monitor for serotonin syndrome symptoms especially in first weeks of combination

effect: Major INR elevation → serious bleeding
clinical_consequence: TMP-SMX inhibits CYP2C9 (S-warfarin metabolism) and displaces warfarin from protein binding. INR increases of 50-100% documented within 3-5 days. High incidence of serious bleeding events reported.
timing_notes: Reduce warfarin dose by 25-50%; monitor INR every 2-3 days during antibiotic course

effect: Severe bone marrow suppression → infection, death
clinical_consequence: Azathioprine is metabolized by xanthine oxidase to inactive metabolites. Allopurinol potently inhibits xanthine oxidase, causing azathioprine accumulation to 400% of normal levels → profound myelosuppression. Fatal cases documented.
timing_notes: If combination necessary, reduce azathioprine dose to 25% of normal and monitor CBC weekly

effect: Colchicine toxicity — multiorgan failure, bone marrow suppression
clinical_consequence: Colchicine is a CYP3A4 and P-gp substrate with a narrow therapeutic index. Clarithromycin inhibits both CYP3A4 and P-gp, dramatically increasing colchicine exposure. Multiple fatal cases of colchicine toxicity documented. Presents as GI distress, myopathy, cytopenias, multiorgan failure.
timing_notes: Avoid combination. If clarithromycin necessary, hold colchicine. Use azithromycin as alternative macrolide.

effect: Metoprolol toxicity — bradycardia, AV block, hypotension
clinical_consequence: Fluoxetine is a potent CYP2D6 inhibitor. Metoprolol is a CYP2D6 substrate with extensive first-pass metabolism. Co-administration increases metoprolol AUC 4-5x in extensive metabolizers. Heart rate of 36-40 bpm documented.
timing_notes: Use lower metoprolol dose; monitor heart rate and BP closely

effect: Paradoxically altered phenytoin levels + increased free fraction → toxicity
clinical_consequence: Valproate displaces phenytoin from albumin (increases free fraction) while also inhibiting phenytoin metabolism. Total phenytoin level may appear low while free (active) level is toxic. Causes nystagmus, ataxia, confusion. Free phenytoin levels essential.
timing_notes: Monitor free (unbound) phenytoin levels, not total levels

effect: Enhanced anticoagulation → increased bleeding risk
clinical_consequence: High-dose vitamin E (>400 IU/day) inhibits vitamin K-dependent carboxylation and may inhibit platelet aggregation. Potentiates warfarin anticoagulant effect. INR elevation documented at doses >800 IU/day.
timing_notes: Limit vitamin E to <200 IU/day if on warfarin; monitor INR

effect: Reduced TKI absorption → treatment failure (cancer)
clinical_consequence: Several TKIs (nilotinib, bosutinib, dasatinib, erlotinib) require acidic gastric environment for dissolution/absorption. PPIs increase gastric pH, reducing bioavailability by 30-60%. May contribute to treatment failure or resistance in oncology patients.
timing_notes: Take TKI 2h before or 10h after PPI; H2 blockers preferred over PPIs. Avoid long-term concurrent use.

effect: GI ulceration, perforation, hemorrhage
clinical_consequence: Additive gastric mucosal damage. Corticosteroids inhibit prostaglandin synthesis and decrease mucus production. NSAIDs inhibit COX-1 protective prostaglandins. Combination increases peptic ulcer risk 15x vs NSAIDs alone. GI bleeds documented.
timing_notes: Use acetaminophen for pain relief. If NSAIDs necessary, add PPI gastroprotection.

effect: Severe prolonged hypoglycemia
clinical_consequence: Alcohol inhibits hepatic gluconeogenesis, impairing the body's ability to counter insulin-induced hypoglycemia. Hypoglycemia may be severe and prolonged (4-12 hours). Alcohol also masks hypoglycemia symptoms. Risk is greatest in fasting state.
timing_notes: Eat with alcohol; monitor blood glucose closely; have glucose available

effect: Disulfiram-like reaction (chlorpropamide) + prolonged hypoglycemia
clinical_consequence: Some sulfonylureas (especially chlorpropamide) inhibit acetaldehyde metabolism → flushing, nausea, tachycardia (disulfiram reaction). All sulfonylureas risk prolonged hypoglycemia with alcohol due to impaired gluconeogenesis. Glyburide particularly risky.
timing_notes: Counsel patients to eat when drinking; avoid alcohol with chlorpropamide specifically

effect: Reduced immunosuppressive efficacy → transplant rejection risk
clinical_consequence: Ashwagandha has immune-stimulating properties that may counteract immunosuppressive therapy. Theoretical risk of transplant rejection or autoimmune flare. May also interact pharmacokinetically via CYP3A4 modulation.
timing_notes: Avoid ashwagandha in transplant patients or those on immunosuppressants

effect: Additive thyroid stimulation → hyperthyroid symptoms
clinical_consequence: Ashwagandha has been shown to increase T3 and T4 levels. Combined with exogenous thyroid hormone may cause supraphysiological thyroid activity: palpitations, tremor, weight loss, anxiety, heat intolerance.
timing_notes: Monitor thyroid function tests (TSH, free T4) when starting/stopping ashwagandha

effect: Enhanced anticoagulation → bleeding risk
clinical_consequence: High-dose garlic inhibits platelet aggregation (ajoene) and may weakly inhibit CYP2C9. Case reports of INR elevation. Risk is dose-dependent; dietary garlic is low risk, but concentrated supplements (>2g allicin equivalent) are concerning.
timing_notes: Monitor INR if garlic supplement use changes; avoid high-dose garlic supplements

effect: Additive antiplatelet effect → increased bleeding risk
clinical_consequence: Ginkgo inhibits platelet-activating factor (PAF), increasing bleeding time. Multiple case reports of spontaneous bleeding (subdural hematoma, subarachnoid hemorrhage) in patients taking ginkgo alone. Combined with warfarin or aspirin significantly increases hemorrhagic risk.
timing_notes: Discontinue 2 weeks before surgery; avoid with anticoagulants

effect: Immune stimulation counteracting immunosuppressive therapy
clinical_consequence: Echinacea stimulates macrophage activity and T-cell proliferation. Theoretical antagonism of immunosuppressive drugs (cyclosporine, tacrolimus, prednisone). Particularly concerning in organ transplant recipients and autoimmune patients.
timing_notes: Avoid in transplant patients; do not use during acute autoimmune flares

effect: Additive CNS depression → excessive sedation, respiratory depression
clinical_consequence: Valerian acts as a GABA-A receptor modulator similar to benzodiazepines. Additive sedation with other CNS depressants. Risk of oversedation, falls in elderly, respiratory depression with opioids.
timing_notes: Avoid combining valerian with any sedating agent

effect: Additive hepatotoxicity → acute liver failure
clinical_consequence: Kava causes idiosyncratic hepatotoxicity (FDA warning issued). Combined with other hepatotoxic drugs greatly increases liver failure risk. Multiple cases of acute liver failure requiring transplant. Banned in several European countries.
timing_notes: Do not combine kava with any hepatotoxic agent or in patients with hepatic disease

effect: Enhanced anticoagulation → bleeding risk
clinical_consequence: Curcumin inhibits platelet aggregation and may inhibit CYP2C9. Therapeutic doses of turmeric supplements (not dietary spice) may enhance anticoagulant effects. INR elevation reported. Risk greater with high-dose supplements.
timing_notes: Avoid high-dose curcumin supplements; monitor INR if using

effect: Additive blood glucose lowering → hypoglycemia
clinical_consequence: Berberine has demonstrated blood glucose-lowering effects comparable to metformin in some trials (AMPK activation). Combined use can cause additive hypoglycemia, especially in type 2 diabetics on insulin or sulfonylureas.
timing_notes: Monitor blood glucose closely when adding berberine to diabetes regimen

effect: Hypertensive crisis — potentially fatal
clinical_consequence: MAOIs prevent MAO-mediated tyramine metabolism. Dietary tyramine causes massive catecholamine release → hypertensive crisis (BP >220/140 mmHg), severe headache, intracranial hemorrhage, death. 'Cheese reaction' is classic. Diet must be strictly restricted.
timing_notes: Strict low-tyramine diet required throughout MAOI treatment and 14 days after stopping

effect: Additive HMG-CoA reductase inhibition → severe myopathy, rhabdomyolysis
clinical_consequence: Red yeast rice naturally contains monacolin K, chemically identical to lovastatin. Combined with prescription statins creates additive HMG-CoA reductase inhibition — effectively doubling statin dose. Severe myopathy and rhabdomyolysis documented.
timing_notes: Discontinue red yeast rice if prescribed a statin

effect: Chelation → reduced antibiotic absorption (up to 31% reduction)
clinical_consequence: Zinc chelates tetracyclines in GI tract. Doxycycline absorption reduced by 31-40% when taken concurrently with zinc. May cause antibiotic failure in treating serious infections.
timing_notes: Separate doxycycline and zinc by at least 2 hours

effect: Increased benzodiazepine levels → prolonged sedation, respiratory depression
clinical_consequence: Grapefruit inhibits CYP3A4. Triazolam AUC increases 50%; midazolam AUC increases 50-100%. Prolonged and deeper sedation. Risk of oversedation and respiratory depression, especially in elderly.
timing_notes: Avoid grapefruit juice with triazolam and midazolam; lorazepam and oxazepam are unaffected

effect: INR elevation → increased bleeding risk
clinical_consequence: Cranberry juice may inhibit CYP2C9 (primary warfarin-metabolizing enzyme). Multiple case reports of INR elevation and bleeding events. Mechanism debated but evidence sufficient for FDA advisory. Large volumes (>1 L/day) pose greatest risk.
timing_notes: Limit cranberry juice consumption; monitor INR closely

effect: Hypomagnesemia → cardiac arrhythmias, seizures, hypocalcemia
clinical_consequence: Long-term PPI use (>1 year) impairs active magnesium absorption in the colon. Hypomagnesemia (Mg <0.7 mmol/L) causes muscle spasms, tremor, seizures, cardiac arrhythmias. FDA safety communication issued. Magnesium does not correct without stopping PPI.
timing_notes: Check serum magnesium before starting long-term PPI; monitor annually

effect: Significantly increased myopathy and rhabdomyolysis risk
clinical_consequence: Untreated hypothyroidism independently increases myopathy risk. Combined with statins, risk multiplies. Hypothyroid patients prescribed statins must have their thyroid function optimized first. TSH should be normalized before statin initiation.
timing_notes: Treat hypothyroidism first; use lowest effective statin dose; monitor CK levels

effect: Worsening hypertension and edema; reduced antihypertensive efficacy
clinical_consequence: NSAIDs inhibit renal prostaglandins → sodium/water retention → edema → elevated BP. Can increase systolic BP by 3-5 mmHg on average. Blunts efficacy of all antihypertensive classes. Particularly problematic with diuretics and ACE inhibitors.
timing_notes: Use acetaminophen for pain management in hypertensive patients

effect: Dysglycemia — both hypoglycemia and hyperglycemia
clinical_consequence: Fluoroquinolones stimulate insulin secretion (hypoglycemia risk with secretagogues) and can block insulin release (hyperglycemia). Gatifloxacin has been withdrawn due to severe glycemic dysregulation. Hypoglycemia was more common with levofloxacin/ciprofloxacin.
timing_notes: Monitor blood glucose closely; patient education about hypoglycemia symptoms

effect: Acute kidney injury, hyperkalemia, drug accumulation
clinical_consequence: In severe CKD (eGFR <30), ACE inhibitors can reduce GFR further due to loss of efferent arteriole angiotensin II-mediated constriction. Also risk of hyperkalemia. However, in CKD 1-3, ACE inhibitors are nephroprotective — risk-benefit assessment required.
timing_notes: Reduce dose for eGFR <30; monitor creatinine and potassium within 1-2 weeks of initiation or dose change

effect: Anticholinergic syndrome — confusion, urinary retention, constipation, delirium
clinical_consequence: Diphenhydramine has significant anticholinergic activity. Combined with other anticholinergics (oxybutynin, tricyclic antidepressants, antipsychotics) creates excessive anticholinergic burden, particularly dangerous in elderly. Beers Criteria lists diphenhydramine as potentially inappropriate in elderly.
timing_notes: Use loratadine or cetirizine (non-sedating antihistamines) as alternatives in elderly patients

effect: GI bleeding — particularly upper GI hemorrhage
clinical_consequence: SNRIs deplete platelet serotonin (required for aggregation). NSAIDs inhibit COX-1 platelet thromboxane. Combination increases upper GI bleed risk 15x vs SSRIs alone and >30x vs neither drug. Effect applies to all SSRIs/SNRIs.
timing_notes: Add PPI gastroprotection (omeprazole 20mg) if combination unavoidable

effect: DOAC accumulation → major bleeding
clinical_consequence: Ketoconazole is a combined P-gp and CYP3A4 inhibitor. All DOACs (apixaban, rivaroxaban, edoxaban) are dual P-gp and CYP3A4 substrates. Apixaban AUC increases 2x; rivaroxaban AUC increases 2.6x. Risk of major bleeding.
timing_notes: Use alternative antifungal if possible (fluconazole has less interaction with apixaban)

effect: Major bleeding risk — GI hemorrhage, intracranial bleeding
clinical_consequence: Additive anticoagulant and antiplatelet effects. Heparin affects clotting factors; aspirin inhibits platelet function and thromboxane synthesis. Combined use in post-surgical or MI patients significantly increases major bleeding events.
timing_notes: If combination required (e.g., ACS), use lowest effective aspirin dose (75-100mg); monitor closely

effect: Lithium toxicity — tremor, confusion, renal failure
clinical_consequence: ACE inhibitors reduce angiotensin II-mediated aldosterone secretion → increased proximal tubular sodium reabsorption → compensatory lithium retention. Lithium levels can rise 30-50% within 1-2 weeks.
timing_notes: Monitor lithium levels within 1 week of starting ACE inhibitor; reassess after dose changes

effect: Unopposed alpha-mediated vasoconstriction → hypertensive crisis, coronary spasm
clinical_consequence: Cocaine blocks norepinephrine reuptake → alpha AND beta stimulation. Beta-blockade leaves alpha stimulation unopposed → severe vasoconstriction, coronary artery spasm, MI, stroke. This applies to topical ophthalmic cocaine use in patients on beta-blockers.
timing_notes: In cocaine toxicity, use benzodiazepines not beta-blockers; consider phentolamine

effect: DXM toxicity — hallucinations, serotonin syndrome features
clinical_consequence: Fluoxetine potently inhibits CYP2D6, the primary DXM-metabolizing enzyme. DXM accumulates → dissociative/hallucinogenic effects (high plasma DXM). Also additive serotonergic effects from DXM's weak SNRI activity.
timing_notes: Use guaifenesin without DXM as cough suppressant alternative

effect: Serotonin syndrome — hyperthermia, neuromuscular abnormalities, death
clinical_consequence: Linezolid is a reversible, non-selective MAOI. Combined with SSRIs creates significant serotonin excess. FDA black box warning. Serotonin syndrome onset within 24-72 hours. Fatal cases documented.
timing_notes: If linezolid necessary, stop SSRI 2 weeks before (5 weeks for fluoxetine). Wait 24h after linezolid before restarting SSRI.

effect: Unpredictable INR — both increase and decrease documented
clinical_consequence: Acute alcohol: inhibits CYP2C9 → increases warfarin levels → supratherapeutic INR. Chronic alcohol: induces CYP2C9 → decreases warfarin levels → subtherapeutic INR → thrombosis. Additional risk: falls, trauma, GI bleeding. Highly unpredictable INR.
timing_notes: Advise strict alcohol avoidance; if patient drinks, monitor INR very frequently

effect: Chelation → dramatically reduced bisphosphonate absorption
clinical_consequence: Calcium (and any polyvalent cation) chelates bisphosphonates in GI tract. Bisphosphonate bioavailability is already only 1-5% under ideal conditions. Any calcium at the time of dosing further reduces absorption, reducing efficacy for osteoporosis treatment.
timing_notes: Take bisphosphonate with plain water on empty stomach; wait at least 30 min before any food, drink, or supplements

effect: INR elevation → bleeding
clinical_consequence: Regular acetaminophen (>2g/day for several days) can increase INR by 1-2 points in warfarin patients, possibly via CYP2C9 or vitamin K cycle inhibition. Effect is dose-dependent. Single doses are generally safe; chronic/high doses are concerning.
timing_notes: Limit acetaminophen to <2g/day with warfarin; monitor INR

effect: Contraceptive failure
clinical_consequence: Enzyme-inducing AEDs dramatically induce CYP3A4, reducing ethinylestradiol and progestin levels by 40-60%. Breakthrough bleeding and contraceptive failure well-documented. Effect begins within days of AED initiation.
timing_notes: Use non-hormonal contraception OR high-dose combined OCP (50mcg ethinylestradiol) if AED cannot be changed
high_risk_groups: women of childbearing age with epilepsy

effect: Chelation → reduced antibiotic absorption
clinical_consequence: Divalent (Ca2+) and trivalent (Al3+, Fe3+) cations chelate tetracyclines. Milk reduces doxycycline absorption by ~20-30%. Effect less severe than with older tetracyclines like tetracycline HCl. May cause treatment failure in serious infections.
timing_notes: Take doxycycline 1 hour before or 2 hours after dairy; take with food if GI upset occurs

effect: Serotonin syndrome + opioid toxicity — hyperthermia, seizures, respiratory failure, death
clinical_consequence: Most dangerous drug interaction in clinical medicine. MAOIs inhibit meperidine metabolism (CYP2B6) and prevent breakdown of serotonin/catecholamines. Meperidine has serotonergic activity. Result: hyperthermia, rigidity, diaphoresis, cardiovascular collapse. Fatalities within minutes.
timing_notes: Absolute contraindication. Use morphine cautiously as alternative. 14-day washout for MAOI required before meperidine.

effect: Rebound hypertensive crisis on clonidine withdrawal
clinical_consequence: Beta-blockers block clonidine-induced alpha-2 mediated sympatholysis withdrawal protection. Abrupt clonidine discontinuation in patients on beta-blockers causes severe rebound hypertension due to unopposed alpha-adrenergic stimulation. Clonidine must be tapered gradually.
timing_notes: Taper clonidine slowly; stop beta-blocker several days BEFORE stopping clonidine if discontinuing both

effect: Theoretical reduced OCP efficacy
clinical_consequence: Historical concern that broad-spectrum antibiotics reduce gut flora that recycle estrogen (enterohepatic circulation). FDA and most guidelines now consider this interaction unproven for most antibiotics except enzyme inducers. Limited evidence for clinical significance but continues to be counseled.
timing_notes: Backup contraception is advisable during antibiotic course as a precaution

effect: Pseudotumor cerebri (benign intracranial hypertension) — severe headache, vision loss
clinical_consequence: Both tetracyclines and retinoids independently cause pseudotumor cerebri. Combination dramatically increases risk of this potentially blinding intracranial hypertension syndrome. Cases of permanent vision loss documented.
timing_notes: Absolute contraindication. Never combine these drug classes.

effect: Binding → severely reduced levothyroxine absorption
clinical_consequence: Cholestyramine is a bile acid sequestrant that binds many drugs in the GI tract. Reduces levothyroxine absorption by 60-70%. Causes clinical hypothyroidism. Same interaction applies to colestipol.
timing_notes: Take levothyroxine 4-6 hours before cholestyramine

effect: Additive QT prolongation → Torsades de Pointes → ventricular fibrillation
clinical_consequence: Hydroxychloroquine blocks hERG potassium channels. Combined with other QT-prolonging drugs creates additive or synergistic QT prolongation. FDA warning issued during COVID-19 pandemic regarding this combination (chloroquine + azithromycin). Life-threatening arrhythmia risk.
timing_notes: ECG monitoring mandatory if combination unavoidable; correct electrolytes

effect: Reduced tamoxifen efficacy → increased breast cancer recurrence
clinical_consequence: Tamoxifen requires CYP2D6-mediated conversion to endoxifen (active metabolite). Paroxetine is one of the most potent CYP2D6 inhibitors. Reduces endoxifen levels by ~65%. Associated with significantly increased breast cancer mortality and recurrence in retrospective studies.
timing_notes: Use venlafaxine or gabapentin for hot flashes instead of paroxetine in tamoxifen patients

effect: Dramatically reduced levodopa efficacy → worsening Parkinsonism
clinical_consequence: Vitamin B6 is a cofactor for DOPA decarboxylase, which converts levodopa to dopamine in the periphery. High-dose B6 >200mg/day dramatically increases peripheral levodopa conversion, reducing CNS-available drug. Note: this interaction is much less significant when carbidopa is combined with levodopa (carbidopa blocks peripheral decarboxylase).
timing_notes: Avoid high-dose B6 with levodopa monotherapy; standard multivitamin doses generally safe with carbidopa-levodopa

effect: INR elevation → major bleeding
clinical_consequence: Ciprofloxacin is a moderate CYP1A2 inhibitor and may also affect gut flora producing vitamin K2. INR increases of 50-100% documented within 3-5 days. Consistent and clinically significant interaction; major bleeding events reported.
timing_notes: Reduce warfarin dose by 25-33%; monitor INR every 2-3 days during antibiotic course

effect: Disulfiram-like reaction — flushing, nausea, vomiting, tachycardia, hypotension
clinical_consequence: Metronidazole inhibits acetaldehyde dehydrogenase → acetaldehyde accumulation when alcohol consumed. Reaction includes severe flushing, headache, nausea, vomiting, sweating, tachycardia, hypotension. Can last 30-60 minutes. Effect persists for 48h after last metronidazole dose.
timing_notes: No alcohol during metronidazole therapy and for 48 hours after last dose

effect: Major INR elevation → potentially fatal bleeding
clinical_consequence: Azole antifungals are potent CYP2C9 and CYP3A4 inhibitors. S-warfarin (primary anticoagulant enantiomer) is CYP2C9 substrate. Voriconazole can increase warfarin AUC >3-fold. INR may exceed 10 within days.
timing_notes: Reduce warfarin by 50%; monitor INR daily for first week

effect: Steven-Johnson syndrome, severe hypersensitivity reactions
clinical_consequence: Combination associated with increased risk of severe cutaneous adverse reactions including Stevens-Johnson syndrome and toxic epidermal necrolysis, particularly in patients with renal impairment. Mechanism may involve impaired immune function in renal disease patients.
timing_notes: Monitor for rash; discontinue both drugs immediately if severe cutaneous reaction develops

effect: Vitamin A toxicity — hepatotoxicity, pseudotumor cerebri, bone toxicity
clinical_consequence: Isotretinoin is a synthetic retinoid (vitamin A derivative). Additional vitamin A supplementation creates additive retinoid toxicity: severe headache (pseudotumor cerebri), hepatotoxicity, bone pain, alopecia, mucocutaneous toxicity. Even multivitamins with vitamin A are concerning at high doses.
timing_notes: Avoid all vitamin A supplements during isotretinoin therapy; use vitamin A-free supplements

effect: Severe hyperkalemia → cardiac arrhythmias, cardiac arrest
clinical_consequence: Both classes reduce aldosterone effect → reduced renal potassium excretion. Combination (unless in carefully monitored heart failure) causes dangerous hyperkalemia (K+ >6 mEq/L). Despite evidence for benefit in heart failure (spironolactone + ACE inhibitor), routine combination requires very close monitoring.
timing_notes: Monitor K+ weekly × 1 month, monthly × 3 months, then quarterly; target K+ <5.5 mEq/L

effect: Fatal opioid toxicity — respiratory arrest
clinical_consequence: Fentanyl is exclusively CYP3A4 metabolized. Potent CYP3A4 inhibitors increase fentanyl AUC dramatically. With ritonavir, fentanyl AUC increases >2-fold. For transdermal patches already delivering continuous opioid exposure, adding CYP3A4 inhibitor can cause fatal respiratory depression without warning.
timing_notes: If combination unavoidable, reduce fentanyl dose, increase monitoring frequency, have naloxone available

effect: Increased risk of gastrointestinal bleeding and ulceration
clinical_consequence: Concurrent use of ibuprofen and alcohol significantly raises the risk of gastric mucosal damage, peptic ulcers, and GI hemorrhage
timing_notes: Avoid alcohol during the entire course of ibuprofen therapy; risk persists as long as both are present
high_risk_groups: Patients with history of peptic ulcer disease

effect: Ibuprofen competitively inhibits aspirin's irreversible binding to COX-1, reducing aspirin's cardioprotective antiplatelet effect
clinical_consequence: Regular ibuprofen use may attenuate low-dose aspirin's cardioprotective effect in patients requiring antiplatelet therapy
timing_notes: Take aspirin at least 30 minutes before or 8 hours after ibuprofen to preserve aspirin's antiplatelet effect
high_risk_groups: Patients on antiplatelet therapy for cardiovascular disease

effect: NSAIDs reduce prostaglandin-mediated renal vasodilation, precipitating acute kidney injury in patients with compromised renal function
clinical_consequence: Use of naproxen in patients with chronic kidney disease can cause acute decompensation, fluid retention, and accelerated decline of renal function
timing_notes: Contraindicated in patients with eGFR <30 mL/min/1.73 m²; use with caution when eGFR 30-60
high_risk_groups: Patients on diuretics or ACE inhibitors

effect: Additive CNS depression including excessive sedation, impaired psychomotor function, and respiratory depression
clinical_consequence: Combined use causes profound sedation, increased risk of falls, and potentially dangerous respiratory depression, particularly in the elderly
timing_notes: Avoid alcohol for the entire duration of diphenhydramine use; effects are additive and can persist for hours
high_risk_groups: Patients with COPD or sleep apnea

effect: Anticholinergic effects cause mydriasis and decreased aqueous humor drainage, raising intraocular pressure
clinical_consequence: Diphenhydramine can precipitate acute angle-closure glaucoma in susceptible patients, causing severe eye pain, blurred vision, and potential permanent vision loss
timing_notes: Contraindicated in angle-closure glaucoma; use with caution in open-angle glaucoma only under ophthalmologist guidance

effect: Anticholinergic activity reduces bladder detrusor contractility, leading to urinary retention
clinical_consequence: Diphenhydramine can cause acute urinary retention in men with BPH, requiring catheterization
timing_notes: Avoid use in patients with BPH; symptoms may develop within hours of first dose

effect: Sympathomimetic effect causes vasoconstriction and increased heart rate, elevating blood pressure
clinical_consequence: Pseudoephedrine can cause significant and potentially dangerous blood pressure elevation in hypertensive patients, increasing risk of hypertensive crisis
timing_notes: Avoid in uncontrolled hypertension; if used in controlled hypertension, monitor BP closely

effect: Sympathomimetic effects are amplified in hyperthyroid state due to upregulated adrenergic receptors
clinical_consequence: Patients with hyperthyroidism are hypersensitive to sympathomimetics; pseudoephedrine can precipitate thyroid storm, severe tachycardia, and hypertension
timing_notes: Contraindicated in uncontrolled hyperthyroidism

effect: Famotidine is renally eliminated; decreased renal function leads to drug accumulation and risk of toxicity including CNS effects
clinical_consequence: Patients with CKD may experience confusion, agitation, and headache due to accumulation of famotidine
timing_notes: Reduce dose and/or increase dosing interval in renal impairment (eGFR <50 mL/min); use 50% dose reduction for eGFR <30

effect: Calcium and antacid alkalinity reduce iron absorption by forming insoluble complexes and raising gastric pH
clinical_consequence: Concurrent use can reduce iron absorption by up to 50%, compromising treatment of iron deficiency anemia
timing_notes: Take iron supplements at least 2 hours before or 4 hours after antacids

effect: Bismuth subsalicylate contains salicylate; concurrent aspirin use leads to additive salicylate toxicity
clinical_consequence: Combined use increases risk of salicylate toxicity including tinnitus, nausea, vomiting, and in severe cases, metabolic acidosis
timing_notes: Avoid concurrent use; if both are needed, monitor for signs of salicylate toxicity

effect: Clarithromycin inhibits CYP3A4 and P-glycoprotein, causing significant increase in loperamide plasma levels and CNS penetration
clinical_consequence: Elevated loperamide concentrations can lead to serious cardiac arrhythmias (QT prolongation, Torsades de Pointes) and respiratory depression
timing_notes: Avoid concurrent use; if necessary, use minimum effective dose of loperamide with cardiac monitoring

effect: Triple interaction: alcohol worsens acetaminophen hepatotoxicity, enhances doxylamine sedation, and increases CNS depression with DXM
clinical_consequence: Severe and potentially fatal combination leading to liver damage, profound sedation, and respiratory depression; a common cause of unintentional overdose
timing_notes: Do not consume alcohol while taking NyQuil or for several hours after last dose

effect: Hepatic metabolism of loratadine is impaired in liver disease, leading to drug accumulation and prolonged half-life
clinical_consequence: Increased loratadine plasma levels may cause adverse effects; dose adjustment needed to prevent toxicity
timing_notes: Reduce dose to 10 mg every other day in patients with significant hepatic impairment

effect: Cetirizine is primarily renally excreted; reduced kidney function leads to drug accumulation and prolonged sedation
clinical_consequence: Patients with renal impairment may experience excessive drowsiness, dizziness, and prolonged pharmacological effects
timing_notes: Reduce dose to 5 mg once daily for CrCl 11-31 mL/min; avoid in patients on hemodialysis

effect: Zinc chelates fluoroquinolones in the GI tract, forming insoluble complexes that markedly reduce antibiotic absorption
clinical_consequence: Reduced ciprofloxacin bioavailability (up to 40%) may lead to subtherapeutic levels and treatment failure of bacterial infections
timing_notes: Take ciprofloxacin at least 2 hours before or 6 hours after zinc supplements
high_risk_groups: Patients being treated for serious bacterial infections
Patients with urinary tract or respiratory infections

effect: High-dose zinc competitively inhibits intestinal copper absorption, potentially causing copper deficiency
clinical_consequence: Prolonged high-dose zinc supplementation can cause copper deficiency anemia, neutropenia, and neurological manifestations; serum copper and ceruloplasmin levels may be falsely low
timing_notes: Monitor serum copper and ceruloplasmin with long-term high-dose zinc use (>40 mg/day for >8 weeks)
high_risk_groups: Patients using high-dose zinc for extended periods

effect: High-dose vitamin C may interfere with warfarin metabolism; may also affect INR test results
clinical_consequence: Very high doses of vitamin C (>1 g/day) may reduce warfarin's anticoagulant effect, increasing thrombotic risk; can also falsely alter INR readings
timing_notes: Monitor INR when initiating, changing, or discontinuing high-dose vitamin C in patients on warfarin
high_risk_groups: Patients using megadose vitamin C (>1 g/day)

effect: Melatonin may inhibit CYP1A2, potentially increasing warfarin plasma concentrations and anticoagulant effect
clinical_consequence: Increased warfarin levels may elevate bleeding risk; INR may increase
timing_notes: Monitor INR when starting, changing dose, or stopping melatonin in patients on warfarin
high_risk_groups: Patients with sleep disorders on anticoagulation

effect: Melatonin may lower the seizure threshold; conflicting evidence on whether it can be proconvulsant or anticonvulsant
clinical_consequence: In some epilepsy patients, melatonin use may alter seizure control; interactions with antiepileptic drugs are possible
timing_notes: Use with caution in epilepsy; discuss with neurologist before use

effect: Magnesium chelates bisphosphonates in the GI tract, significantly reducing their oral bioavailability
clinical_consequence: Reduced alendronate absorption leads to subtherapeutic drug levels, compromising osteoporosis treatment efficacy
timing_notes: Take bisphosphonates on an empty stomach 30 minutes before any minerals or supplements; avoid magnesium within 2 hours of bisphosphonate dosing
high_risk_groups: Postmenopausal women on bisphosphonates for osteoporosis

effect: Thiazides reduce urinary calcium excretion; vitamin D increases intestinal calcium absorption; together may cause hypercalcemia
clinical_consequence: Hypercalcemia can cause kidney stones, nephrocalcinosis, confusion, constipation, and cardiac arrhythmias
timing_notes: Monitor serum calcium levels periodically when high-dose vitamin D is used with thiazide diuretics
high_risk_groups: Patients on thiazides with high-dose vitamin D (>2000 IU/day)

effect: Metformin impairs vitamin B12 absorption in the ileum by inhibiting calcium-dependent absorption mechanisms
clinical_consequence: Long-term metformin use causes B12 deficiency in up to 30% of patients, potentially leading to megaloblastic anemia and peripheral neuropathy
timing_notes: Monitor serum B12 annually in patients on long-term metformin; supplement B12 if levels decline

effect: Alpha lipoic acid has insulin-sensitizing properties and may enhance glucose-lowering effects, increasing hypoglycemia risk
clinical_consequence: Combined use may cause hypoglycemia; blood glucose monitoring is essential during concurrent use
timing_notes: Monitor blood glucose levels closely when initiating or adjusting alpha lipoic acid in diabetic patients on insulin
high_risk_groups: Type 2 diabetics on insulin or oral hypoglycemics

effect: Green tea contains vitamin K which antagonizes warfarin; EGCG may also inhibit platelet aggregation, enhancing bleeding risk
clinical_consequence: Variable effects on INR; large amounts may reduce warfarin efficacy while platelet inhibition adds bleeding risk
timing_notes: Monitor INR closely when initiating or changing green tea extract dosing; avoid large variable quantities

effect: Silymarin may inhibit CYP3A4 and OATP1B1, potentially increasing statin plasma concentrations
clinical_consequence: Modest increase in statin levels may elevate risk of myopathy; effect is generally clinically insignificant at typical supplement doses
timing_notes: Monitor for signs of myopathy (muscle pain, weakness) with concurrent use

effect: Dong quai contains coumarins with anticoagulant properties and may inhibit platelet aggregation, potentiating warfarin's anticoagulant effect
clinical_consequence: Significantly increased INR and risk of serious bleeding events including GI hemorrhage and intracranial bleeding
timing_notes: Avoid concurrent use; if transition is needed, allow 2 weeks and monitor INR closely
high_risk_groups: All patients on warfarin or anticoagulants
Postmenopausal women using dong quai for hot flashes

effect: Black cohosh has documented hepatotoxic potential; liver disease increases vulnerability to hepatotoxic effects
clinical_consequence: Cases of severe hepatitis, liver failure, and death have been associated with black cohosh use, especially in patients with pre-existing liver disease
timing_notes: Contraindicated in patients with liver disease; discontinue at first sign of liver dysfunction (jaundice, right upper quadrant pain)

effect: Gamma-linolenic acid in evening primrose oil may lower seizure threshold and interact with antiepileptic drugs
clinical_consequence: Risk of breakthrough seizures, including temporal lobe epilepsy; potential for seizure induction in previously controlled patients
timing_notes: Avoid in patients with seizure disorders or those at risk for seizures

effect: Saw palmetto may inhibit platelet aggregation through inhibition of thromboxane A2, potentiating anticoagulant effect
clinical_consequence: Increased bleeding risk; case reports of excessive bleeding with concurrent anticoagulation
timing_notes: Monitor INR closely; discontinue saw palmetto at least 2 weeks before any surgical procedure
high_risk_groups: Men on anticoagulants taking saw palmetto for BPH symptoms

effect: CoQ10 is structurally similar to vitamin K2 and may reduce warfarin's anticoagulant effect via similar mechanisms
clinical_consequence: Reduced INR and decreased anticoagulant effect; risk of thromboembolism if anticoagulation is inadequate
timing_notes: Monitor INR when starting or stopping CoQ10; dose adjustment of warfarin may be necessary
high_risk_groups: Heart failure patients on CoQ10 and anticoagulation

effect: 5-HTP increases serotonin synthesis; combined with SSRI serotonin reuptake inhibition causes excessive serotonergic activity
clinical_consequence: Risk of serotonin syndrome: agitation, hyperthermia, tachycardia, diaphoresis, clonus, and in severe cases, death
timing_notes: Avoid concurrent use; allow 2 weeks washout period when transitioning between these agents

effect: Tryptophan is a serotonin precursor; with MAO inhibition, serotonin accumulates excessively
clinical_consequence: Life-threatening serotonin syndrome with high fever, agitation, muscle rigidity, and cardiovascular collapse
timing_notes: Contraindicated; do not use tryptophan supplements within 14 days of MAOI therapy
high_risk_groups: Patients with Parkinson's disease on selegiline

effect: SAMe may augment serotonergic activity; combined with SSRIs can cause excessive serotonin effect
clinical_consequence: Risk of mild to moderate serotonin syndrome; may cause agitation, restlessness, diaphoresis
timing_notes: If used together, start SAMe at low doses and monitor for signs of serotonin toxicity

effect: CBD is a potent inhibitor of CYP2C9 and CYP3A4, which are primary enzymes for warfarin metabolism, leading to elevated warfarin concentrations
clinical_consequence: Significantly elevated warfarin levels and INR, substantially increasing the risk of serious and potentially fatal bleeding events
timing_notes: Avoid concurrent use; if used together, reduce warfarin dose significantly and monitor INR very frequently

effect: Kratom alkaloids (mitragynine) act as partial opioid agonists; combined with opioid analgesics causes profound CNS and respiratory depression
clinical_consequence: Life-threatening respiratory depression; risk of opioid overdose death; multiple fatalities reported with kratom-opioid combinations
timing_notes: Contraindicated; do not use kratom with any opioid analgesic
high_risk_groups: Patients with sleep apnea or COPD

effect: Resveratrol inhibits CYP2C9, decreasing warfarin metabolism and increasing anticoagulant effect
clinical_consequence: Elevated warfarin levels increase bleeding risk; INR may rise significantly
timing_notes: Monitor INR when initiating or changing resveratrol dose in patients on warfarin
high_risk_groups: Patients on warfarin or other vitamin K antagonists

effect: Psyllium may bind lithium in the GI tract, reducing its absorption and plasma levels
clinical_consequence: Reduced lithium levels may lead to therapeutic failure in bipolar disorder management
timing_notes: Take lithium at least 2 hours before or 4 hours after psyllium supplements
high_risk_groups: Patients with bipolar disorder on lithium

effect: Glucosamine (especially with chondroitin) may potentiate warfarin's anticoagulant effect through unclear mechanisms, possibly affecting vitamin K metabolism
clinical_consequence: Increased INR and bleeding risk; case reports of INR exceeding 8 with concomitant use
timing_notes: Monitor INR closely when initiating or changing glucosamine/chondroitin in patients on warfarin
high_risk_groups: Elderly patients with arthritis on anticoagulation

effect: L-arginine is a nitric oxide precursor causing vasodilation; in heart failure, this may cause excessive hypotension and may increase mortality
clinical_consequence: Post-MI patients with heart failure who took L-arginine had increased mortality in clinical trials; worsening heart failure possible
timing_notes: Avoid L-arginine supplementation in patients with heart failure, particularly post-MI

effect: DHEA has immunomodulatory effects; may alter disease activity in autoimmune conditions
clinical_consequence: In SLE, DHEA may modestly reduce disease flares, but can also interact with immunosuppressive therapy and affect hormone-sensitive symptoms
timing_notes: Use under specialist supervision; monitor disease activity and drug interactions with corticosteroids and immunosuppressants
high_risk_groups: Women with SLE on hormone-sensitive therapies

effect: Lion's mane may inhibit platelet aggregation, potentially enhancing anticoagulant effects
clinical_consequence: Increased bleeding risk when combined with anticoagulants; effect magnitude is uncertain
timing_notes: Monitor for bleeding signs; check INR if using warfarin; discontinue before surgery
high_risk_groups: Patients on anticoagulants or antiplatelet therapy

effect: Antibiotics kill probiotic organisms if taken simultaneously, rendering the probiotic supplement ineffective
clinical_consequence: Probiotic supplementation during antibiotic therapy may be less effective; however, taking them at least 2 hours apart preserves probiotic benefit in preventing antibiotic-associated diarrhea
timing_notes: Take probiotics at least 2 hours after antibiotic dose; continue probiotics for 1-2 weeks after completing antibiotic course
high_risk_groups: Patients with history of C. difficile infection

effect: L-carnitine may potentiate warfarin anticoagulant effect through CYP2C9 inhibition
clinical_consequence: Elevated INR and increased bleeding risk reported in case studies
timing_notes: Monitor INR closely when initiating or discontinuing L-carnitine in patients on warfarin
high_risk_groups: Patients with dialysis-related carnitine deficiency on anticoagulation

effect: NSAIDs including ibuprofen can trigger oxidative stress; in G6PD-deficient patients with impaired antioxidant defense, this can trigger hemolytic anemia
clinical_consequence: Acute hemolytic anemia with pallor, jaundice, dark urine, and fatigue
timing_notes: Avoid ibuprofen and other NSAIDs in patients with G6PD deficiency if possible; use acetaminophen as safer alternative (at standard doses)
high_risk_groups: Mediterranean, African, Asian ancestry populations with G6PD deficiency

effect: Aspirin can induce oxidative stress, triggering hemolysis in G6PD-deficient red blood cells
clinical_consequence: Dose-dependent hemolytic anemia; high-dose aspirin poses significant risk in G6PD deficiency
timing_notes: Avoid high-dose aspirin; low-dose (81 mg/day) may be used with caution in some G6PD deficiency variants under physician supervision
high_risk_groups: Patients with severe G6PD deficiency variants
Patients with G6PD deficiency class I or II

effect: Miconazole potently inhibits CYP2C9, severely reducing warfarin metabolism and causing drug accumulation
clinical_consequence: Dramatically elevated warfarin levels and INR; serious and life-threatening bleeding events have been reported, even with topical/oral gel formulations
timing_notes: Contraindicated; even buccal or topical miconazole is absorbed sufficiently to cause this interaction; use alternative antifungal
high_risk_groups: All patients on warfarin or acenocoumarol

effect: Clotrimazole inhibits CYP3A4, reducing tacrolimus metabolism and significantly elevating tacrolimus blood levels
clinical_consequence: Tacrolimus toxicity including nephrotoxicity, neurotoxicity, and increased risk of rejection paradoxically from toxicity-driven dose reductions
timing_notes: Avoid concurrent use; if necessary, monitor tacrolimus trough levels very closely and adjust dose accordingly
high_risk_groups: Transplant patients on tacrolimus prophylaxis for oral candidiasis

effect: Phenylephrine's sympathomimetic effects are markedly potentiated by MAO inhibition, causing severe and potentially fatal hypertensive crisis
clinical_consequence: Hypertensive crisis with severe headache, intracranial hemorrhage, and potentially death
timing_notes: Contraindicated; avoid for at least 14 days after stopping MAOI

effect: Grapefruit, orange, and apple juices significantly reduce fexofenadine bioavailability by inhibiting OATP1A2 intestinal transporters
clinical_consequence: Fexofenadine plasma levels can be reduced by up to 36-47%, potentially reducing antihistamine efficacy
timing_notes: Take fexofenadine with water; avoid grapefruit, orange, or apple juice within 4 hours of dosing
high_risk_groups: Patients relying on fexofenadine for allergy control

effect: Omeprazole is a known porphyrinogenic agent that can induce heme synthesis pathway enzymes, triggering acute porphyria attacks
clinical_consequence: Acute porphyria attack with severe abdominal pain, neurological manifestations, cardiovascular instability, and potential death
timing_notes: Contraindicated in patients with acute porphyria; use safer alternatives such as ranitidine (with caution) or sucralfate
high_risk_groups: Women during menstrual cycle with porphyria

effect: High-dose guaifenesin can cause false-positive results for urinary vanillylmandelic acid (VMA) and 5-HIAA on colorimetric assay tests
clinical_consequence: False-positive urine catecholamine metabolite tests may lead to unnecessary workup for pheochromocytoma or carcinoid tumor
timing_notes: Discontinue guaifenesin 48-72 hours before urine VMA or 5-HIAA laboratory testing
high_risk_groups: Patients undergoing evaluation for pheochromocytoma or carcinoid syndrome

effect: Low-dose aspirin reduces uric acid excretion by competing for renal tubular secretion; high-dose aspirin increases uric acid excretion (uricosuric effect)
clinical_consequence: Low-dose aspirin (<2 g/day) falsely elevates serum uric acid; high-dose aspirin can lower uric acid. This can confound gout diagnosis and monitoring
timing_notes: Document aspirin dose and timing when interpreting serum uric acid results; ideally hold aspirin 5-7 days before testing
high_risk_groups: Patients with gout or hyperuricemia on cardiovascular low-dose aspirin

effect: Acetaminophen at doses >2 g/day inhibits vitamin K-dependent carboxylation and reduces clotting factor synthesis, elevating INR
clinical_consequence: Falsely elevated INR may lead to unnecessary warfarin dose reductions; actual bleeding risk also increases at higher acetaminophen doses with warfarin
timing_notes: Monitor INR closely when acetaminophen is used regularly in patients on warfarin; note dose of acetaminophen on lab request
high_risk_groups: Patients on warfarin taking regular acetaminophen for pain
Elderly patients with chronic pain on anticoagulation

effect: High-dose biotin interferes with biotin-streptavidin immunoassay technology used in many laboratory tests including troponin, TSH, T4, and D-dimer
clinical_consequence: Falsely low troponin results may cause missed MI diagnosis; falsely abnormal TSH may lead to incorrect thyroid disorder treatment
timing_notes: Discontinue biotin supplements for at least 24-72 hours before troponin, TSH, T4, or hormone lab testing
high_risk_groups: Patients taking high-dose biotin (>5 mg/day) for hair loss or MS
Patients presenting with chest pain symptoms

effect: NSAIDs reduce renal prostaglandin production and renal blood flow, leading to genuine or artifactual elevations in serum creatinine
clinical_consequence: Elevated creatinine may reflect true NSAID-induced nephrotoxicity or may confound assessment of underlying renal function
timing_notes: Hold NSAIDs before renal function testing if possible; note NSAID use when interpreting creatinine results

effect: High-fat meals increase labetalol bioavailability by approximately 40% by enhancing first-pass metabolism avoidance
clinical_consequence: Increased labetalol plasma levels may cause excessive blood pressure lowering, bradycardia, and dizziness when taken with fatty food
timing_notes: Take labetalol consistently either with or without food; avoid high-fat meals if inconsistency in dosing effect is observed

effect: At high doses or transdermal delivery, selegiline loses MAO-B selectivity; tyramine cannot be broken down by intestinal MAO, causing massive norepinephrine release
clinical_consequence: Hypertensive crisis (cheese reaction): sudden severe hypertension, severe headache, risk of stroke or MI
timing_notes: Avoid tyramine-rich foods with transdermal selegiline patches ≥9 mg/24h and high-dose oral selegiline; standard 5-10 mg oral doses for Parkinson's may have less restriction
high_risk_groups: Patients on high-dose selegiline or transdermal selegiline for depression

effect: Polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons from charcoal-broiled food induce CYP1A2, increasing clozapine metabolism and reducing plasma concentrations
clinical_consequence: Reduced clozapine levels may lead to breakthrough psychotic symptoms; abrupt dietary changes can cause significant fluctuations in drug levels
timing_notes: Encourage consistent diet; monitor for changes in psychiatric symptoms with significant dietary changes; recheck clozapine levels if diet changes substantially

effect: Ciprofloxacin inhibits CYP1A2, reducing caffeine metabolism by up to 60% and significantly prolonging caffeine half-life
clinical_consequence: Caffeine accumulation causes excessive CNS stimulation: anxiety, insomnia, palpitations, tremors, and headache
timing_notes: Advise patients to reduce caffeine intake significantly during ciprofloxacin treatment; effects persist throughout antibiotic course

effect: Glycyrrhizin inhibits 11-beta-hydroxysteroid dehydrogenase, causing pseudoaldosteronism with sodium retention, potassium loss, and hypertension
clinical_consequence: Blood pressure increases significantly despite antihypertensive therapy; hypokalemia may cause cardiac arrhythmias
timing_notes: Avoid excessive licorice consumption (>50 g/day) with antihypertensive therapy; effect is dose-dependent and can develop over weeks

effect: Additive CNS depression through potentiation of GABA-A receptor activity and general membrane fluidization effects
clinical_consequence: Profound sedation, respiratory depression, coma, and death; synergistic effect far exceeds each drug alone
timing_notes: Avoid alcohol throughout benzodiazepine therapy; risk is greatest with acute alcohol use coinciding with benzodiazepine peak levels
high_risk_groups: Patients with COPD or sleep apnea

effect: NSAIDs impair renal prostaglandin-mediated sodium excretion, causing fluid retention and increased cardiac preload; also reduce efficacy of diuretics and ACE inhibitors
clinical_consequence: Acute decompensation of heart failure with edema, dyspnea, and hospitalisation; increased mortality risk
timing_notes: Contraindicated in heart failure; use acetaminophen for pain as safer alternative
high_risk_groups: All patients with systolic or diastolic heart failure

effect: Anticholinergic agents interfere with acetylcholine-mediated neuromuscular transmission, worsening the neuromuscular blockade in myasthenia gravis
clinical_consequence: Worsening muscle weakness, difficulty swallowing, respiratory muscle weakness, and potential myasthenic crisis requiring mechanical ventilation
timing_notes: Contraindicated; avoid all anticholinergic medications in myasthenia gravis; use non-anticholinergic antihistamines (loratadine, cetirizine) instead

effect: Naproxen is extensively hepatically metabolized; liver disease impairs clearance and causes drug accumulation with risk of GI bleeding and hepatotoxicity
clinical_consequence: Increased risk of GI bleeding, hepatotoxicity, and fluid retention in patients with cirrhosis or severe hepatic impairment
timing_notes: Avoid NSAIDs in patients with significant hepatic impairment (Child-Pugh B or C); use with caution only in mild liver disease

effect: Long-term PPI use (>3 months) impairs magnesium absorption in the intestine, causing hypomagnesemia
clinical_consequence: Hypomagnesemia (low serum magnesium) can cause tetany, seizures, arrhythmias, and potentiate hypokalemia; serum magnesium testing may reveal drug effect
timing_notes: Check serum magnesium before starting long-term PPI therapy and every 3-6 months; supplement if levels fall below normal
high_risk_groups: Patients on long-term PPI therapy (>3 months)

effect: Hepatic disease reduces glutathione stores and may alter acetaminophen metabolism, increasing NAPQI accumulation and hepatotoxicity
clinical_consequence: Risk of acute liver failure at lower-than-usual doses; however, acetaminophen is still preferred over NSAIDs in liver disease if used cautiously
timing_notes: Limit to 2 g/day (maximum) in patients with liver disease; avoid entirely in severe hepatic impairment (Child-Pugh C)

effect: Aspirin-exacerbated respiratory disease (AERD) - aspirin inhibits COX-1, shunting arachidonic acid to leukotriene pathway, causing bronchoconstriction
clinical_consequence: Aspirin-induced bronchospasm in susceptible COPD patients; approximately 10-20% of COPD patients may have AERD overlap
timing_notes: Test for aspirin sensitivity before use; if AERD confirmed, avoid all NSAIDs or perform supervised aspirin desensitization
high_risk_groups: COPD patients with nasal polyps or aspirin sensitivity

effect: Caffeine competitively blocks adenosine receptors, antagonizing the vasodilatory effect of adenosine used in pharmacological cardiac stress testing
clinical_consequence: False-negative cardiac stress test results; coronary artery disease may be missed due to inadequate coronary vasodilation
timing_notes: Avoid all caffeine-containing foods and beverages for at least 12-24 hours before pharmacological stress testing with adenosine or regadenoson
high_risk_groups: Patients undergoing cardiac stress testing for coronary artery disease evaluation

effect: High-dose vitamin C can interfere with glucose oxidase-based point-of-care blood glucose meters by acting as a reducing agent, causing falsely low glucose readings
clinical_consequence: Falsely low glucose readings can lead to inappropriate insulin dosing and life-threatening hypoglycemia in diabetic patients
timing_notes: Avoid blood glucose testing within 2 hours of high-dose vitamin C ingestion; use laboratory hexokinase-based methods for accurate glucose measurement
high_risk_groups: Diabetic patients using home blood glucose meters
ICU patients on high-dose IV vitamin C

effect: Bismuth and salicylate accumulate in renal failure; salicylate inhibits prostaglandin-mediated renal vasodilation, worsening renal function
clinical_consequence: Salicylate toxicity and further kidney function deterioration in patients with CKD
timing_notes: Avoid bismuth subsalicylate in patients with CKD stages 3-5; use alternative antidiarrheal agents such as loperamide

effect: Pregnenolone and its metabolites modulate GABA-A and NMDA receptors; may alter seizure threshold
clinical_consequence: Theoretical risk of altered seizure control; limited clinical data but caution warranted in patients with epilepsy
timing_notes: Avoid in patients with seizure disorders; discuss with neurologist before use
high_risk_groups: Patients with epilepsy on antiepileptic drugs

effect: Tyrosine is a catecholamine precursor; MAO inhibition prevents breakdown of dopamine and norepinephrine synthesized from tyrosine, causing pressor effects
clinical_consequence: Hypertensive crisis: severe hypertension, intense headache, and risk of stroke or MI
timing_notes: Avoid tyrosine supplements within 14 days of MAOI therapy
high_risk_groups: Patients on selegiline for Parkinson's disease

effect: High-protein collagen supplements increase nitrogen load; in CKD, reduced renal clearance of nitrogenous waste products accelerates kidney disease progression
clinical_consequence: Worsening of renal function parameters (elevated BUN, creatinine) in patients with CKD on high-dose collagen supplements
timing_notes: Monitor renal function closely; limit protein intake per nephrologist guidance in CKD; avoid high-dose collagen supplements in advanced CKD

effect: CYP2D6 poor metabolizers cannot efficiently convert DXM to its inactive metabolite dextrorphan, leading to DXM accumulation
clinical_consequence: Elevated DXM levels cause dissociative symptoms, hallucinations, and psychomotor impairment even at standard doses
timing_notes: Use minimum effective dose in CYP2D6 poor metabolizers; start at 50% of standard dose

effect: Lycopene has mild antioxidant-mediated vasodilatory properties; may have additive blood pressure-lowering effects
clinical_consequence: Possible mild additive hypotensive effect; generally well tolerated but monitor blood pressure in sensitive individuals
timing_notes: Monitor blood pressure when initiating high-dose lycopene supplementation in patients on antihypertensives
high_risk_groups: Patients with labile or poorly controlled hypertension
Elderly patients prone to orthostatic hypotension

effect: Metoclopramide is a central dopamine D2 receptor antagonist; blocks dopamine receptors, severely worsening Parkinson's symptoms
clinical_consequence: Dramatic worsening of Parkinsonism, akinetic crisis, severe rigidity, and risk of neuroleptic malignant syndrome
timing_notes: Contraindicated in Parkinson's disease; use domperidone (peripheral dopamine antagonist, where available) or ondansetron as alternatives for nausea

effect: NSAIDs can cause aseptic meningitis as a hypersensitivity reaction in SLE patients; also increase cardiovascular and renal risks common in SLE
clinical_consequence: Risk of aseptic meningitis, worsening lupus nephritis, and exacerbation of hypertension in SLE patients
timing_notes: Use with caution and only for short periods; monitor renal function and blood pressure; use lower doses when possible

effect: Prolonged PPI use suppresses gastric acid, impairing release of B12 from food proteins and reducing B12 absorption dependent on intrinsic factor-gastric acid interaction
clinical_consequence: Serum B12 levels may fall into deficient range with long-term PPI use; neurological and hematological manifestations of B12 deficiency may occur
timing_notes: Check serum B12 annually in patients on long-term PPI therapy (>2 years); supplement with crystalline B12 which does not require gastric acid for absorption
high_risk_groups: Elderly patients on long-term PPI therapy
Patients with prior low-normal B12 levels

effect: High-fat meal significantly increases ivermectin bioavailability (by approximately 2.5-fold) compared to fasting state
clinical_consequence: Increased ivermectin exposure may enhance efficacy against parasitic infections, but also increases risk of dose-related adverse effects including dizziness, hypotension, and neurological effects
timing_notes: For parasitic infections requiring standard dosing, take with water on empty stomach; note if high-fat food changes efficacy or side effect profile
high_risk_groups: Elderly patients with reduced P-glycoprotein function

effect: Naproxen is listed as a porphyrinogenic drug; may induce heme synthesis enzymes and trigger acute attacks in susceptible patients
clinical_consequence: Acute porphyria attack with severe abdominal pain, neuropathy, and psychiatric symptoms
timing_notes: Avoid in patients with known acute porphyria; consult porphyria drug database before prescribing
high_risk_groups: Women of reproductive age with porphyria

effect: Even standard therapeutic doses of acetaminophen can transiently elevate serum ALT and AST levels in some patients, independent of clinical liver injury
clinical_consequence: Transient ALT/AST elevation may be misinterpreted as hepatotoxicity, leading to unnecessary medication withdrawal or hepatology referral
timing_notes: Document acetaminophen use on lab order; hold acetaminophen 24-48 hours before liver enzyme testing if feasible
high_risk_groups: Patients fasting or with reduced nutritional status

effect: Acute alcohol intoxication raises seizure threshold temporarily, but alcohol withdrawal causes rebound CNS hyperexcitability and lowers seizure threshold
clinical_consequence: Alcohol withdrawal seizures in alcohol-dependent epilepsy patients; also reduces antiepileptic drug efficacy; chronic alcohol use induces CYP enzymes reducing AED levels
timing_notes: Patients with epilepsy should abstain from alcohol; alcohol withdrawal should be managed medically to prevent seizures
high_risk_groups: Patients with epilepsy on antiepileptic drugs
Patients with alcohol use disorder and seizure history

effect: Excess iodine from kelp or iodine supplements can paradoxically worsen hypothyroidism (Wolff-Chaikoff effect) or cause hyperthyroidism in Graves' disease
clinical_consequence: Worsened thyroid function requiring adjustments to levothyroxine dosing; risk of thyrotoxicosis in patients with autonomous thyroid nodules
timing_notes: Avoid high-iodine supplements in patients with thyroid disorders; monitor TSH closely if used

effect: Phenylephrine stimulates alpha-1 adrenergic receptors in the bladder neck and prostate, increasing smooth muscle tone and urethral resistance
clinical_consequence: Worsening of urinary symptoms including urinary retention, incomplete bladder emptying, and acute urinary obstruction in men with BPH
timing_notes: Avoid phenylephrine and other alpha-agonist decongestants in men with BPH; use antihistamines without decongestants or intranasal corticosteroids instead

effect: NSAIDs reduce renal prostaglandin synthesis, impairing renin-angiotensin-aldosterone axis, leading to potassium retention and hyperkalemia
clinical_consequence: Genuinely elevated serum potassium that may confound assessment; risk of cardiac arrhythmias in susceptible patients; must distinguish from specimen hemolysis
timing_notes: Monitor serum potassium and renal function when patients on medications prone to hyperkalemia (ACE-I, ARBs, potassium-sparing diuretics) begin NSAID therapy
high_risk_groups: Patients with CKD on ACE inhibitors or ARBs

effect: Diphenhydramine's anticholinergic activity provides some symptomatic relief of tremor but also causes confusion, urinary retention, constipation, and paradoxical movement worsening in Parkinson's disease
clinical_consequence: Significant anticholinergic toxicity including delirium, hallucinations, and urinary retention; may worsen non-motor symptoms of Parkinson's; also worsens dementia in DLB
timing_notes: Avoid diphenhydramine and all anticholinergic antihistamines in Parkinson's disease; use loratadine or cetirizine for allergy symptoms

effect: High-dose vitamin D (>4000 IU/day) increases intestinal calcium absorption and bone resorption, raising serum calcium levels
clinical_consequence: Hypercalcemia detected on lab testing may represent true vitamin D toxicity; symptoms include nausea, polyuria, nephrolithiasis, and cardiac arrhythmias
timing_notes: Monitor serum calcium and 25-OH vitamin D levels when supplementing with doses >2000 IU/day; note supplement use on lab orders
high_risk_groups: Patients taking high-dose vitamin D supplementation

effect: Additive negative chronotropic and inotropic effects; risk of severe bradycardia, heart block, and cardiac failure
clinical_consequence: Combination can cause life-threatening bradycardia, AV block, hypotension, and acute decompensated heart failure
timing_notes: Avoid concurrent use; if necessary, initiate at lowest doses with continuous cardiac monitoring

effect: Synergistic depression of SA and AV node conduction leading to bradycardia and AV block
clinical_consequence: High-degree AV block, symptomatic bradycardia, hypotension; may precipitate heart failure
timing_notes: Use only when clinically indicated; monitor ECG, heart rate, and blood pressure closely

effect: Amiodarone inhibits CYP2D6, increasing carvedilol plasma levels; combined bradycardia and hypotension risk
